ontology.foodex2_description: Muffins — sweet baked goods made with flour, eggs, fat, and sugar; banana walnut variant
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers muffins as a sweet baked good category; no banana-walnut-specific sub-code exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for banana walnut muffin; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard banana walnut muffin weighing approximately 120 g, as commonly sold at Singaporean cafés and bakeries.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Muffins, blueberry, commercially prepared (SR Legacy NDB 18275) used as structural analogue; banana and walnut nutrient contributions cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for banana raw (NDB 09040) and walnuts English (NDB 12155)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry for banana walnut muffin exists. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy commercially prepared muffin data (energy ~350 kcal/100g, protein ~5.4g, fat ~16.7g, carbohydrate ~45.8g) and cross-validated against the input record's figures, which align closely with USDA muffin analogues. Input values retained and validated against USDA SR Legacy. Micronutrients (calcium 50mg, iron 1.25mg, vitamin C 1.67mg, cholesterol 37.5mg, trans fat 0.08g, saturated fat 2.92g) are consistent with USDA banana, walnut, and commercial muffin component data. Detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, potassium, and moisture data are not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy muffin entry consulted; those fields are set to -1 accordingly.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Muffin (blueberry, commercially prepared) GI ~59–65 used as analogous estimate for banana walnut muffin.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for banana walnut muffin found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from commercially prepared muffins (GI range 59–65 per Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Banana contributes natural sugars that may elevate GI slightly above plain muffin; walnut fat content moderates postprandial glucose response. GI 65 (upper bound of muffin range) selected as conservative estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100 = 65 × 55.0 / 100 = 35.75 ≈ 35.8 (serving = 120g; carbohydrate per serving = 45.83 × 1.20 = 55.0g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (25g/100g) with low dietary fibre (2.08g/100g) and borderline sodium (291.67mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit banana walnut muffins due to high sugar content (25g/100g) exceeding the solid-food sugar threshold (>10g/100g with fibre <2g/100g), borderline sodium at 292mg/100g, and insufficient potassium or fibre offset; if consumed, limit to half a muffin (60g) and pair with a high-fibre, low-sodium meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (25g/100g) and high refined carbohydrate (45.83g/100g) with low fibre (2.08g/100g) producing a high glycaemic load (~35.8 per standard serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Banana walnut muffins are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to sugar at 25g/100g (far exceeding the 15g/100g diabetes threshold), a high glycaemic load of approximately 35.8 per 120g serving, and low dietary fibre (2.08g/100g); substituting refined flour with wholemeal or almond flour and reducing added sugar to ≤5g/100g would be required before this food could be considered appropriate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.92g/100g), trans fat present (0.08g/100g), high sugar (25g/100g), and cholesterol (37.5mg/100g); walnuts provide cardioprotective omega-3 ALA
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume banana walnut muffins only occasionally and in small portions (≤60g), as the combination of trans fat (0.08g/100g), saturated fat (2.92g/100g), high sugar (25g/100g), and moderate cholesterol (37.5mg/100g) presents a cumulative cardiovascular risk, partially offset by the omega-3 ALA content of walnuts; a reformulated version with reduced sugar, no trans fat, and increased walnut proportion would be more appropriate.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (25g/100g) exceeds the 15g/100g RA refined sugar threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory mediators despite walnut omega-3 ALA content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid banana walnut muffins in their current formulation as sugar at 25g/100g drives inflammatory mediator production; a reformulated recipe with sugar reduced below 10g/100g and substantially increased walnut content could shift the inflammatory balance toward a more neutral or anti-inflammatory profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banana Walnut Muffin is a Western-style sweet baked good widely available at Singaporean cafés, bakeries, and food delivery platforms. It is made by combining mashed ripe bananas, chopped walnuts, wheat flour, eggs, butter, sugar, baking powder, and salt into a batter that is portioned into muffin tins and baked until golden. The result is a moist, dense, and slightly sweet muffin with a tender crumb, pronounced banana flavour, and crunchy walnut pieces distributed throughout. The ripe banana provides natural sweetness and moisture, while the walnuts contribute a rich, slightly bitter nuttiness and a satisfying textural contrast. Per 100g, the muffin delivers approximately 350 kcal, 5.42g protein, 16.67g fat (2.92g saturated, 0.08g trans), and 45.83g carbohydrate including 25g sugar and 2.08g dietary fibre, with sodium at 291.67mg. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium-high), producing a high glycaemic load of approximately 35.8 per standard 120g serving. Clinically, the high sugar content (25g/100g) disqualifies this food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid arthritis under standard dietary thresholds. The walnut component provides omega-3 alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), which has anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties, but is insufficient to offset the high sugar and refined carbohydrate load in a typical muffin recipe. The food is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten, eggs, dairy, and tree nuts (walnuts) as major allergens. It is best consumed as an occasional treat rather than a regular dietary staple.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a banana walnut muffin?
Can diabetics eat banana walnut muffin?
Is banana walnut muffin suitable for hypertension?
What is the glycemic index of banana walnut muffin?
Does banana walnut muffin contain gluten?
How much sugar is in a banana walnut muffin?
Banana walnut muffin calories per piece

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry for banana walnut muffin exists. Nutrient values from the input record (grabfood-sourced, confidence 0.7) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy commercial muffin analogues and have been validated against component-level USDA data for banana and walnuts. GI is an analogous estimate from the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care and indexed by the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match and the absence of a direct primary-source entry for this specific product.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, potassium, and moisture data for this specific banana walnut muffin formulation are not individually reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy commercially prepared muffin entry (NDB 18275) or the Singapore HPB Food Composition Database as of 2024; these fields are therefore set to the -1 sentinel value indicating data not available from consulted approved sources. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.08g/100g per input source, consistent with trace trans fat from commercial baking fats; this value is retained as supported by USDA SR Legacy muffin data. All core macros (protein_g 5.42, fat_g 16.67, carbohydrate_g 45.83) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a baked muffin product. GI estimated at 65 from Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283) using commercially prepared muffin as analogous food; banana natural sugars may elevate GI slightly, walnut fat content moderates postprandial response. Glycaemic load per 120g serving = 65 × (45.83 × 1.20) / 100 = 65 × 55.0 / 100 ≈ 35.8. This food is classified as a Western-style baked good sold in Singapore; multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish multilingual requirements.
